items.title: [正面] Let me go ahead and address the biggest concern pe...
items.summary:
Let me go ahead and address the biggest concern people seem to have have when it comes to getting into Morimens: "HEY, DOESN'T THIS USE AI?!"

The only thing Studio B.I.A.V. has used AI for is to translate the very much human-written and human-illustrated story, [b]and even then they've been hard at work alongside their volunteer translators to completely redo it with human translation[/b]. It was so they would have an easy means of releasing Morimens in the west and gauging interest, and something they're rectifying now that they have the means. [b]As of the 2nd Anniversary update on 11/17/2025, the majority of the game's story now has a human translation![/b] You can even find a special thanks in-game to all the talented people who volunteered their skills to make that happen. Willing to give Morimens a shot now? Then let me tell you the pros and cons of this game. I do hope you'll try it, and maybe by the end of things you'll come to love it as much as I do.

[h2]The Good[/h2]
- The art of Morimens is absolutely [i]gorgeous[/i]. I really can't sing the praises of the art team enough. And it was really kind of Studio B.I.A.V. to make all of the backgrounds and CGs available as you unlock them in the game's gallery. Each character, even the freebies, stands out with an unforgettable design.

- The soundtrack can also be unlocked in-game, but it's also available for listening on Spotify. And it's so good that I find myself listening to it even during moments where I'm not playing the game. Deeply emotional in all its forms, helping to amplify whichever scene is playing out. From tragic scenes that make you feel like your heart's been torn out, to the hope spots and shenanigans that help you smile afterwards.

- Look, I'll be the first to admit I may be a little biased as a fan of all things cosmic horror. But it's a breath of fresh air to not only see it done, but to also see a team putting their own unique spin on the genre. There are certainly quite a few references to the works of H.P. Lovecraft, but Studio B.I.A.V. is doing their best to craft a unique setting while including their own takes on what makes cosmic horror truly "cosmic". The horror aspects of the story aside, even with its current translation we're given the pleasure of getting to know many well-written characters that you'll find yourself getting very attached to.

- For a gacha game, Morimens is actually pretty generous. You can pay real money for rolls, of course, but I've been fairly comfortably free-to-play without needing to spend money on pulls. The pity system it has works well, and the sediment shop gives you the ability to continue powering up your limited-time awakeners even after their banners leave. The only things that are hard-paywalled seem to be skins, which...if you're gonna paywall anything, it may as well be cosmetics.

- As someone who struggles with deck-builder games at times, Morimens is very hands-on with its teaching. While the difficulty curve gets pretty steep in later parts of the story, and indeed for some events, you won't struggle with understanding the basics. So by the time you get to these steep curves in the difficulty, you'll likely know what to do to overcome them.

[h2]The Bad[/h2]
- There's a lot of currencies to keep up with. Silver, silver prime, pure cores, luminous cores, rose scrip, sediment, investigation badges, D-jewels, black offerings, and the items associated with whatever event is going on. It can be a bit much.

- I'm conflicted about putting this here, but the use of stamina in this game is...odd. You don't have to spend anything to play through the story, but you do in order to get the materials needed to strengthen your awakeners. That in of itself is fine, but the problem is when it comes to the events. Morimen's limited events cost SO much stamina to do, and if you want to get all the limited time things from it, that better be what you're using all your stamina on! Of course there are things like item synthesis and the rose scrip shop that help, but I find myself frequently going broke in that category because of it. Because not only do you need rose scrips to buy things from the store, you also need them for every synthesis, upgrade, and ascension. And those are far from cheap!

- The above can make it hard for you to overcome some of the difficulty spikes this game throws at you, making it feel like you have to take breaks for days on end just to grind up and prepare for that one fight you've been having a hard time on. I know roguelikes aren't supposed to be easy, but still. The fact that many of the things you can utilize to make your team stronger are level-locked can also be quite frustrating.

[h2]The...Eh. (Your Mileage May Vary)[/h2]
- Morimens is still a gacha game. I'm aware that Studio B.I.A.V. has got to pay the bills, and I'm personally not bothered by it considering how generous the game is already. But I also understand that this can be a turn-off for a lot of people. Pity system or no, there may be characters or Wheels of Destiny you just won't get sometimes, and there's not much you can do besides pray that it isn't a limited-time awakener.

- I said earlier that the only things that were hard paywalled were skins. That's still mostly true, but occasionally the game will give you these little packages for some reason or another. Many of them are filled with helpful goodies you can still get by other means. The first two items are free, but in order to get the next two free items, you have to get past one item that's locked by the cash-exclusive silver prime currency. Annoying, but easily ignoreable.

items.title: [正面] Disregard the play time, I have about 100+ hours p...
items.summary:
Disregard the play time, I have about 100+ hours playing this on mobile.

This game scratches the Slay the Spire itch. I'm not a fan of microtransaction games, but I'd merrily pay $20-30 for Morimens as a full game
